data.title: Best and worst performing S&P 500 stocks in a rosy year for index
data.description:
The S&P 500 had an outstanding 2024, gaining over 20% over the year and for the second consecutive year. The last time it did so was 27 years ago.

The index was driven by Fed rate cuts, the U.S. Presidential election and an investing frenzy in AI. Easing inflation indicators and other favorable macroeconomic reports – including jobs and consumption figures – further buoyed the market.

Consequently, the tech sector was a big winner in the S&P 500 index, with Nvidia (NVDA) being arguably the biggest of them with a gain of around +178%. Others in the Magnificent 7 also had a stellar 2024, with Microsoft (MSFT) +13%, Apple (AAPL) +31%, Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL) +37%, Amazon (AMZN) +46%, and Meta Platforms (META) and Tesla (TSLA) +67%.

Communication services (XLC) made the largest gain among S&P 500 sectors, rising nearly 34% over 2024. The financial sector had a solid year too, increasing almost 28%. Consumer discretionary and information technology sectors were both up 26% and 24%, respectively.

Looking ahead to 2025, analysts appear most positive about the communication, energy and information technology sectors.

Here were the 10 best-performing stocks from the index in 2024:

  • Palantir Technologies (PLTR): 2024 growth +356.15%
  • Vistra (VST): 2024 growth +262.15%
  • Nvidia (NVDA): 2024 growth +178.8%
  • GE Vernova (GEV): 2024 growth +150.61%
  • United Airlines (UAL): 2024 growth +138.46%
  • Axon Enterprise (AXON): 2024 growth +136.19%
  • Broadcom (AVGO): 2024 growth +133.60%
  • Texas Pacific Land (TPL): 2024 growth +106.77%
  • Targa Resources (TRGP): 2024 growth +106.55%
  • Howmet Aerospace (HWM): 2024 growth +103.55%

Here are the 10 worst-performing S&P 500 stocks in 2024:

  • Walgreens Boots Alliance (WBA): -64.99%
  • Moderna (MRNA): -63%
  • Intel (INTC): -58%
  • Celanese (CE): -55.27%
  • The Estée Lauder Companies (EL): -48.45%
  • Enphase Energy (ENPH): -47.67%
  • Dollar Tree (DLTR): -47.43%
  • Humana (HUM): -46.03%
  • Dollar General (DG): -46.01%
  • CVS Health (CVS): -44.48%

More on the biggest S&P 500 movers of 2024

  • Dollar Tree Stores: Short-Term Concerns But Still A Buy
  • Moderna: From Breakthrough COVID-19 Vaccines To Personalized Cancer Therapies
  • Palantir: Applaud The Fundamentals, But Short Valuation
  • Cerence AI spikes on collab with Nvidia to advance LLMs
  • Oracle, Snowflake next up among top AI software plays in 2025: Wedbush

data.title: Viking Therapeutics among potential buyout candidates: Oppenheimer
data.description:
Ahead of the 2025 J.P. Morgan Healthcare Conference scheduled to take place in a few days, Oppenheimer named five potential buyout candidates in biotech on Friday, naming obesity drug developer Viking Therapeutics (NASDAQ:VKTX) in the list.

In addition to VKTX, Biegler included Blueprint Medicines (NASDAQ:BPMC), Janux Therapeutics (NASDAQ:JANX), Arcellx (NASDAQ:ACLX), and Nuvalent (NUVL) in the group.

"The second week of January tends to be a bellwether for biotech M&A and a broader gauge of sector sentiment," analyst Matthew Biegler wrote, as the JPM conference is expected to get underway in San Francisco, California, on January 13-16.

"We need more M&A, and not the garden variety we've gotten since COVID," he added, noting that the biotech sentiment has fallen short of expectations this year.

The analyst said all five stocks are "large enough to make headlines," and an M&A deal or two for them could reverse the current biotech sentiment.

"We think a takeout or two (below) could catalyze a reversal of the XBI's fortunes," Biegler wrote, referring to the industry benchmark, SPDR S&P Biotech ETF (XBI).

With an Outperform recommendation on Viking (NASDAQ:VKTX), the analyst named the company one of the few investable stocks in obesity outside Eli Lilly (LLY), Novo Nordisk (NVO), and Amgen (AMGN).

The San Diego, California-based biotech, which is advancing a promising obesity pill called VK2735, is a leader in the development of oral obesity drugs, Biegler argued, adding that "a takeout would show us that big pharma is willing to pay for a leadership position."

According to Oppenheimer, a large pharma company with operations in immunology and inflammation (I&I) could pursue Blueprint Medicines (NASDAQ:BPMC), which markets Ayvakit, the only FDA-approved treatment for a rare disorder called systemic mastocytosis.

"If SM market is worth $2B, we're not sure why a large pharma hasn't pounced," the analyst wrote with an outperform recommendation on BPMC.

While noting that Janux Therapeutics (JNAX) can be expensive in valuation, the analyst highlighted the potential in its bispecific platform, which he said "can readily be adapted to other targets/cancers—and wouldn't it be nice if big pharma agreed?"

Additionally, the analyst said Arcellx (NASDAQ:ACLX) has what many analysts consider a best-in-class CAR-T therapy for multiple myeloma. According to Biegler, the company has witnessed its valuation peaking as Gilead (GILD) shares ownership for its anito-cel MM therapy. "While a takeout by Gilead would be nice, we think a takeout by someone else would be even better."

On Nuvalent (NUVL), the analyst said the company owns a best-in-class ALK inhibitor, according to Wall Street analysts. "A takeout at current valuations would essentially imply patients staying on drug for years at a time."

data.title: Zymeworks up after sizable purchase by large shareholder
data.description:
• Zymeworks (NASDAQ:ZYME) is up ~3% in Friday trading following the disclosure of a large puirchase of shares by a more than 10% owner.
  • An SEC filing posted Jan. 2 after market close indicates that EcoR1 Capital, which also serves as a director of the company, bought ~363K shares recently in three transactions between $14.47 and $14.78 a share. The purchases total more than $5.3M.
  • EcoR1 now owns ~15.2M shares.

data.title: Freeport in talks with Indonesia government on copper concentrate exports
data.description:
Freeport McMoRan (NYSE:FCX) is in talks with Indonesia's government to continue exports of copper concentrate until its new Manyar smelter resumes full operations, Reuters reported Friday.

Freeport (FCX) had been allowed to export copper concentrate until the end of December, as its new smelter was expected to process its mine production domestically, but the Manyar smelter is currently shut down following a fire in October.

Repairs are ongoing and a ramp-up to operations is now expected to begin around mid-year, which "will certainly have an impact on our concentrate storage capacity both at the Amamapare port and at the PTFI smelter, which will be full going forward," a company spokesperson told Reuters.

Indonesia's Energy and Mineral Resources Minister said the government is considering Freeport's (FCX) request and that a decision will be taken in a meeting with President Subianto.

The Manyar smelter has an input capacity of 1.7M metric tons/year of copper concentrate.

data.title: Beer and spirits investors have a chance to fade the Surgeon General
data.description:
Investors appeared to have taken notice of the Surgeon General's push to add warning labels to alcoholic drinks.

Boston Beer (NYSE:SAM) -3.3%, Anheuser-Busch InBev (BUD) -2.4%, Molson Coors (NYSE:TAP) -2.3%, and Ambev S.A. (NYSE:ABEV) -1.4% were some of the notable brewery stock trading lower in Friday morning action, while spirits giants Diageo (NYSE:DEO) -3.6% and Brown-Forman (NYSE:BF.A) (NYSE:BF.B) -1.0% also traded weak.

In Europe, Pernod Ricard (OTCPK:PDRDF) (OTCPK:PRNDY) swung 2.5% lower and Heineken (OTCQX:HEINY) was down 1.3%. Meanwhile, Davide Campari-Milano N.V. (OTCPK:DVDCF) (OTCPK:DVCMY) was down more than 4.5%.

The Surgeon General issued an advisory report that linked cancer risk to alcohol consumption based on large pools of data. The report indicated direct links between alcohol and at least seven types of cancer, including those of the throat, mouth, esophagus, voice box, colon and liver, as well as more than 16% of breast cancers.

Currently, South Korea is the only major nation that requires a cancer warning on alcohol products. Ireland will require a cancer warning starting in 2026.

For investors, a huge factor to consider is that the decision to update the current warning label has to be made by Congress. Currently, there is no indication that the Trump administration or either party has the political will to issue the alcohol warnings labels. Due to that reality, several sell-side analysts have suggested that the share price decline in beer and spirits stocks is unwarranted.

data.title: Biden eases rules for hydrogen tax credits worth billions
data.description:
The Biden administration loosened some safeguards on a tax credit worth billions of dollars for production of so-called clean hydrogen, Bloomberg reported Friday.

The tax credit created by the Inflation Reduction Act, President Biden's 2022 climate law, now includes a carve-out sought by companies including Constellation Energy (NASDAQ:CEG) and Vistra (NYSE:VST) that will benefit some existing nuclear power plants, according to final rules released by the Treasury Department; CEG +3.5% and VST +6.6%.

The rules also provide pathways for hydrogen made from natural gas with carbon capture systems, methane and renewable natural gas to receive the tax credit, helping lift shares of Plug Power (PLUG) and FuelCell Energy (FCEL), +1.5% and +10.9%, respectively.

Other potentially relevant stocks showing gains include Nuscale Power (SMR) +10.7%, Lightbridge (LTBR) +15.5%, Oklo (OKLO) +16.6%, Centrus Energy (LEU) +6.9%, Nano Nuclear Energy (NNE) +6.3%.

The new rules say that up to 200 MW of a reactor's power generation capacity can be considered new clean power and collect the credits, if they were otherwise at risk of shutting down due to poor economics.

The rules also allow natural gas-fired facilities that produce hydrogen to access the credits if they install equipment to capture and bury their carbon dioxide emissions.

ETFs: (NLR), (URA), (URNM)

data.title: Ford Q4 total sales up 8.8% on gains in EVs, F-Series trucks
data.description:
Led by record sales in EVs, Ford (NYSE:F) sold 8.8% more cars in the fourth quarter, driving 2024 sales up 6%, double the estimated rate of the broader industry.

Retail sales were up 17% in Q4, led by a 25% retail gain in the Ford F-Series pickup truck.

For all of 2024, the company sold 38% more electrified vehicles (HEV, PHEV, and electric) with 30,176 sold in just the final quarter of the year. Each of Ford’s (F) electric vehicles set new sales records in 2024 with Mustang Mach-E sales up 27%, F-150 Lightning sales up 39%, and E-Transit sales up 64%. Hybrid sales were up 40% from 2023 with the F-150 hybrid ending the year as the top-selling hybrid truck in the U.S. Total F-Series Q4 sales were up 21%.

Source: Press Release

data.title: This chart highlights how businesses and consumers dodged the blow of Fed rate hikes
data.description:
The Federal Reserve in 2025 is in the midst of a rate-cutting cycle, but its rapid pace of rate increases in 2022 and 2023 didn’t blunt U.S. business and consumer activity as much as policymakers may have expected.

Corporations largely should have been hit with an increase in debt-servicing costs, and consumers were meant to feel a pocketbook pinch when the Fed, facing an inflation flare-up, jacked up its key interest rate from zero to a range of 5.25%-5.5% from March 2022-July 2023.

But consumers and businesses respectively moved to lock-in low mortgage and borrowing rates. “In short, the transmission mechanism of monetary policy has been much weaker than the economics textbook would have predicted,” Torsten Slok, chief economist at Apollo Global Management, said in a Thursday blog post.

Locked-in low-interest rates alongside strong corporate earnings drove down net interest payments as a share of operating surplus during the hiking cycle and currently sit near a record low, as the Apollo chart shows below:

The rate hikes – 11 in total – did hurt households and companies with weak earnings, revenue, and cash flow, but from an overall macro perspective, the negative effects on corporations and consumers “have been small,” Slok said.

The “economy never slowed down when the Fed raised interest rates. And now the Fed is cutting, boosting asset prices and growth in consumer spending and capex spending further,” Slok said.

In a Friday blog post, he said the Fed’s rate cuts of 100 basis points since September combined with developments in financial markets will boost GDP by 1 percentage point and inflation by 0.5 percentage points over the coming quarters.

U.S. real gross domestic product was contracting when the Fed embarked on rate hikes in March 2022, but the economy avoided a recession. GDP eventually revved higher, and annual growth clocked in at 2.8% in Q3 2024.

In the U.S., the effective mortgage rate is 4%, according to government data, Apollo and Bloomberg, Apollo said. Roughly 86% of U.S. homeowners with mortgages hold a rate below 6%, Redfin said in August.

Treasury yields (US10Y)(US2Y) have recently pushed higher, in part as the Fed raised its 2025 projection for core PCE to 2.5% from 2.2%.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: X, RIVN, CVNA, and more
data.description:
Stock futures edged higher on Friday after a volatile start to 2025, as investors digested economic data showing a slight improvement in manufacturing activity. The U.S. ISM Manufacturing PMI rose to 49.3 in December from 48.4 in November, exceeding the consensus of 48.2, signaling a modest rebound in the sector.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • SuperCom (SPCB) shares extended their rally in premarket trading on Friday, gaining an additional 10% after surging 59% in the previous session. The momentum follows the announcement of two new contracts with key agencies in Kentucky. These contracts involve deploying SuperCom's advanced public safety technologies, aimed at enhancing monitoring capabilities and delivering tailored, reliable solutions.
  • Chewy's (CHWY) shares rose 3% after Wolfe Research upgraded the pet product retailer to Outperform from Peerperform, highlighting that the market may be overlooking its sales growth potential for 2025. Analyst Shweta Khajuria expressed a more favorable outlook on the growth of net sales per active customer (NSPAC), projecting revenue for 2025 to exceed consensus estimates by over 2% and EBITDA by more than 5%. Additionally, Chewy has several growth drivers, including product innovations and international expansion. Wolfe Research considers the stock's valuation "highly reasonable," setting a price target of $42, which suggests a 24% upside from Thursday’s closing price.
  • Rivian Automotive (RIVN) shares jumped 17% after reporting Q4 production of 12,727 vehicles and deliveries of 14,183, both surpassing analyst expectations. For FY2024, the company produced 49,476 vehicles and delivered 51,579, aligning with its guidance range of 47,000–49,000 vehicles produced and 50,500–52,000 delivered. The electric vehicle maker also noted that a previously constrained shared component for its R1 and RCV platforms is no longer an issue.
  • Cerence AI (CRNC) shares soared more than 70% following an expanded collaboration with Nvidia (NVDA) to enhance its large language models (CaLLM) for automotive applications. Using Nvidia AI Enterprise's cloud-native platform, Cerence aims to optimize computing power and implement agentic architecture for its voice generative AI technology, already deployed in over 500M vehicles. The partnership promises cost savings and improved performance for automakers adopting generative AI solutions. Additionally, Cerence recently signed a multi-year deal with JLR to develop next-generation in-car experiences, marking a strong start to the year.

Biggest stock losers

  • Shares of United States Steel (X) plunged over 7% in amid reports that outgoing U.S. President Joe Biden plans to block Nippon Steel Corp.'s proposed $14.1B acquisition. This comes despite Nippon Steel’s offer to grant the U.S. government final authority over U.S. Steel’s production capacity to address White House concerns. While the president has signaled opposition, the White House has not formally confirmed the decision, though an announcement is expected as soon as Friday.
  • Carvana (CVNA) fell as much as 4% after Hindenburg Research published an unfavorable report, confirming it had taken a short position in the company based on its findings. In response, a Carvana spokesperson told Seeking Alpha that "the arguments in today's report are intentionally misleading and inaccurate" and have already been presented by other short sellers looking to benefit from a decline in the stock price. The spokesperson added that the company remains focused on executing its plan for a strong year in 2025.
